title: Shakshuka
subtitle: Eggs poached in spiced tomato sauce
excerpt: Eggs poached directly in a pan of cumin-spiced tomato and pepper sauce until the whites are set and the yolks still runny. A Levantine dish now found everywhere — for good reason. Serves two to four.
sourceNotes: Shakshuka is found across North Africa and the Middle East, with particular association with Israeli and Tunisian home cooking. The dish is documented by Yotam Ottolenghi and Sami Tamimi in Jerusalem (2012) and by Paula Wolfert in The Food of Morocco. Regional versions vary in heat and spicing; this version follows the Levantine approach with cumin and smoked paprika.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sauce can be made up to two days ahead. Reheat until simmering, then add the eggs.

content.text: The sauce needs to reduce to a thick, almost jammy consistency before the eggs go in. If the sauce is too thin, the egg whites spread out and cook unevenly; if the sauce is thick, the eggs sit in it neatly and cook at the same rate. Give the sauce at least 15 minutes before you add the eggs.

The eggs should be covered with a lid immediately after going in, and watched closely. Two minutes gives a set white and a very runny yolk; three minutes gives a slightly more set yolk that still flows when broken. Beyond three minutes, the yolk is cooked through and the dish loses its point.

items.prepNote: crumbled over the top to serve

content.text: A wide frying pan with a lid is essential — the lid traps steam to cook the egg whites without overcooking the yolks. If your pan has no lid, use a large plate or a sheet of foil.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over medium heat. Cook {{onion}} finely chopped onion for 8 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika, and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes; stir for 1 minute. Add the sliced peppers and cook for 5 minutes. Pour in {{tinned-tomatoes}} of hand-crushed tomatoes and season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Simmer uncovered for 15 minutes until the sauce is thick. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Make four wells in the sauce and crack an egg into each one. Cover the pan with a lid and cook for 2–3 minutes until the whites are just set. Scatter over the parsley and crumble over feta if using. Serve immediately, straight from the pan, with bread.

items.symptom: Egg whites still raw but yolks overcooked
items.cause: Heat too high, or eggs added to sauce that was too thin.
items.fix: Reduce the heat to low before adding the eggs. Make sure the sauce is thick enough to hold the eggs in distinct wells.

items.cause: Not simmered long enough, or pan too small so the sauce steams rather than reduces.
items.fix: The sauce needs 15 minutes of active simmering to reduce. Use a wide pan with plenty of surface area.

items.cause: The wells were not made deep enough, so the eggs touched the base of the pan.
items.fix: Push the sauce to the edges to create proper wells. The eggs should be surrounded by sauce on all sides.

content.text: Shakshuka is claimed by multiple culinary traditions (Tunisian, Israeli, Turkish, Libyan) and the dish belongs to all of them. The combination of eggs poached in spiced tomato sauce appears in various forms across North Africa and the Middle East, and the dish has spread from those regions into the breakfast and brunch culture of much of the world over the last two decades. Eaten with good bread to scoop up the sauce, it is one of the most satisfying one-pan dishes there is.
